data.title: The Cortex Job
data.premise: You've taken what seemed like a simple salvage job in the outer rim, but the derelict ship you've boarded contains something the Alliance desperately wants to keep buried. Now you're trapped in a deadly game of cat and mouse with a ruthless Alliance operative while trying to keep your crew alive.
data.central_question: Can you escape the Alliance trap and protect your crew without sacrificing the innocent lives caught in the crossfire?
data.healing_context: Medical supplies and basic field medicine can treat minor injuries within hours. Serious wounds require proper medical equipment aboard Serenity or an Alliance facility. Without treatment, injuries worsen and can become life-threatening within days.
starting_inventory: a pistol with two spare clips

setting.name: Derelict Research Vessel 'Meridian' in the Georgia System
setting.description: A massive Alliance research ship drifts silent and dark in space, its corridors filled with emergency lighting and the hum of failing life support. The ship's research labs contain evidence of horrific experiments on civilians, while hidden Alliance operatives hunt through the shadows.
setting.tone_notes: Claustrophobic tension with moments of dark humor. The familiar Firefly banter contrasts sharply with the sinister discoveries and mounting danger.

acts.title: The Salvage
acts.description: You board the derelict research vessel expecting routine salvage, but discover signs of a cover-up and realize you're not alone on the ship.
acts.end_condition: Discovery of the research data or first contact with Alliance operatives
locked_constraints: research data cannot be fully understood
key_beats: find the first clue about what really happened

acts.title: The Hunt
acts.description: Alliance operatives reveal themselves and begin hunting your crew through the ship while you uncover the full horror of the research project.
acts.end_condition: Major confrontation with Alliance forces or crew member captured
key_beats: first direct encounter with Alliance operatives
discover the true scope of the experiments

acts.title: The Choice
acts.description: With the Alliance closing in and innocent lives at stake, you must choose between saving your crew, protecting the evidence, or preventing future atrocities.
acts.end_condition: Final confrontation resolved and escape attempted
locked_constraints: all major NPCs' fates must be determined
key_beats: final confrontation with the Alliance operative
choose what to do with the research data

npcs.secret: Suspects this job was a setup from the beginning

npcs.name: Commander Sarah Chen

npcs.secret: Is one of the original researchers who helped design the experiments

npcs.name: Dr. Marcus Webb
npcs.motivation: Atone for his role in the experiments by exposing the truth
npcs.secret: Has a hidden copy of all the research data

factions.name: Alliance Special Operations
factions.description: A secretive military unit tasked with covering up the Alliance's illegal human experimentation program
factions.goals: Retrieve all evidence of the experiments and eliminate any witnesses

factions.name: Serenity Crew
factions.description: Independent smugglers and former Browncoats trying to survive in Alliance space
factions.goals: Complete jobs, stay free, and protect each other from Alliance persecution

world_rules: The Alliance will go to extreme lengths to protect its secrets
Space is vast and help is far away
Trust is a luxury that can get you killed
The ship's life support systems are failing and time is running out

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it

- Keep the passage between 150-300 words
- All four choices should feel plausible and interesting
- Never reveal information the skeleton marks as hidden/secret unless the act's end condition has been met
- Weave threat-type status effects into the narrative as approaching dangers — build tension as their timers count down
- Focus on prose quality, atmosphere, character voice, and dramatic tension
- Do NOT include state_updates, health numbers, or mechanical data in your response
- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes
- Introduce key NPCs (antagonists, allies, mentors) naturally throughout each act. Do not wait until the climax to introduce important characters. Check the NPC INTRODUCTION DIRECTIVES section if present.

data.passagePreview: The research vessel Meridian hangs in the black like a metal coffin, her running lights dark and her

data.passage:
The research vessel Meridian hangs in the black like a metal coffin, her running lights dark and her hull scarred by what looks like weapon fire. You float Serenity alongside the massive ship, studying the docking port through the bridge windows. What should have been a simple salvage job—clean out some Alliance tech, sell it on the rim—suddenly feels a mite more complicated.

"Well, that's a sight," Zoe says from behind you, her voice carrying that particular tone that means she's thinking the same thing you are. "Thought this bird was supposed to be empty on account of equipment failure."

Jayne shoulders his way onto the bridge, Vera cradled in his arms like a favorite pet. "Looks plenty empty to me, Mal. Dead as Kaylee's last boyfriend." He grins at his own joke.

"Hey!" Kaylee protests from the engine room comm, her voice tinny but indignant. "Tommy Chen wasn't dead, he was just... resting. A lot."

You key the external scanners, watching the readouts flicker across your screen. Life support's running on emergency power, but there's definitely atmosphere. No heat signatures showing on the scans, but then again, these sensors aren't exactly military grade. The Alliance research ship drifts silent before you, keeping her secrets locked behind blast doors and bureaucratic classifications.

The job came through Badger with his usual lack of useful details—get aboard, grab anything valuable, get out. Simple. Course, simple jobs have a way of turning complicated real quick in your line of work.

A.text: Dock immediately and board with the full crew—safety in numbers
A.consequence: Full crew exposure to unknown dangers, but maximum firepower and skills available
A.narration_directive: The crew boards together through the main docking port. Show their different reactions to the eerie ship interior and hint at hidden dangers.

B.text: Send Kaylee to scan the ship's systems remotely before boarding
B.consequence: Kaylee discovers useful information about ship systems and potential hazards before boarding
B.narration_directive: Kaylee's technical expertise reveals important details about the ship's condition and recent activity. Provide tactical advantage for the boarding.

C.text: Take only Zoe and board quietly through the service airlock
C.consequence: Stealthy approach with Mal and Zoe, avoiding main systems but limiting crew resources
C.narration_directive: Mal and Zoe enter quietly through a service entrance. Emphasize their military partnership and careful reconnaissance of the ship's layout.

D.text: Hail the ship first to see if anyone's actually aboard
D.consequence: Alerts any hidden occupants to Serenity's presence and hostile intentions
D.narration_directive: The hail reveals the ship isn't as empty as it appears. Alliance operatives now know of Serenity's presence and begin preparing countermeasures.

    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

This is the OPENING PASSAGE. No player choice was made yet. Do not apply any negative status effects or penalties — the player has not made any choices yet. time_elapsed should reflect the scene's timeframe.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
The research vessel Meridian hangs in the black like a metal coffin, her running lights dark and her hull scarred by what looks like weapon fire. You float Serenity alongside the massive ship, studying the docking port through the bridge windows. What should have been a simple salvage job—clean out some Alliance tech, sell it on the rim—suddenly feels a mite more complicated.

"Well, that's a sight," Zoe says from behind you, her voice carrying that particular tone that means she's thinking the same thing you are. "Thought this bird was supposed to be empty on account of equipment failure."

Jayne shoulders his way onto the bridge, Vera cradled in his arms like a favorite pet. "Looks plenty empty to me, Mal. Dead as Kaylee's last boyfriend." He grins at his own joke.

"Hey!" Kaylee protests from the engine room comm, her voice tinny but indignant. "Tommy Chen wasn't dead, he was just... resting. A lot."

You key the external scanners, watching the readouts flicker across your screen. Life support's running on emergency power, but there's definitely atmosphere. No heat signatures showing on the scans, but then again, these sensors aren't exactly military grade. The Alliance research ship drifts silent before you, keeping her secrets locked behind blast doors and bureaucratic classifications.

The job came through Badger with his usual lack of useful details—get aboard, grab anything valuable, get out. Simple. Course, simple jobs have a way of turning complicated real quick in your line of work.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Dock immediately and board with the full crew—safety in numbers
B: Send Kaylee to scan the ship's systems remotely before boarding
C: Take only Zoe and board quietly through the service airlock
D: Hail the ship first to see if anyone's actually aboard

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You key the comm, broadcasting on standard frequencies. "Research vessel Meridian, this is the transport ship Serenity. Please respond." Your voice echoes through the bridge as static fills the channel. Zoe raises an eyebrow, her expression clearly saying 'polite as always, sir,' while Jayne snorts. "Real subtle, Mal. Why don't we just send 'em an engraved invitation?"

Kaylee peers at her console, frowning. "That's odd... I'm picking up power signatures from their communication array. More than there should be for a dead ship." The static cuts out abruptly, replaced by an ominous silence. Then, deep within the Meridian's darkened hull, running lights begin flickering to life in sequence.

"Well, gorram," you mutter, watching as the derelict transforms before your eyes. Weapon systems that should be offline start powering up, their targeting arrays sweeping toward Serenity. Zoe's hand moves to her sidearm. "Sir, I'm thinking whoever's over there wasn't exactly planning a welcome party."

Jayne chambers a round. "So much for the element of surprise." The Meridian's comm crackles to life with a cold, professional female voice: "Serenity, you are trespassing in a restricted military zone. Prepare to be boarded for inspection."

A.text: Try to bluff your way out, claiming you're just legitimate salvagers
A.consequence: Bluff might work temporarily but Alliance will remain suspicious and watchful
A.narration_directive: The Alliance operative seems to consider the salvage claim but remains skeptical. Tension builds as they decide whether to believe the story.

B.text: Fire up Serenity's engines and attempt to escape immediately
B.consequence: Immediate weapons fire from the Meridian, causing serious damage to Serenity
B.narration_directive: Serenity's engines roar to life but the Meridian's weapons are faster. Energy blasts tear into the ship's hull as you desperately try to escape.

C.text: Demand to know why an Alliance ship is posing as a derelict
C.consequence: Gains information about Alliance operations without escalating immediate threat
C.narration_directive: Your direct challenge catches the Alliance operative off-guard, revealing more about their mission than they intended to share.

D.text: Cut communications and prepare for a fight while looking for escape routes
D.consequence: Cuts off communication but escalates to combat readiness on both sides
D.narration_directive: The comm goes dead as both ships prepare for battle. The crew scrambles to battle stations while scanning for weaknesses in the Meridian's defenses.

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:

Pre-determined consequence: "Alerts any hidden occupants to Serenity's presence and hostile intentions"
Narration directive: "The hail reveals the ship isn't as empty as it appears. Alliance operatives now know of Serenity's presence and begin preparing countermeasures."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You key the comm, broadcasting on standard frequencies. "Research vessel Meridian, this is the transport ship Serenity. Please respond." Your voice echoes through the bridge as static fills the channel. Zoe raises an eyebrow, her expression clearly saying 'polite as always, sir,' while Jayne snorts. "Real subtle, Mal. Why don't we just send 'em an engraved invitation?"

Kaylee peers at her console, frowning. "That's odd... I'm picking up power signatures from their communication array. More than there should be for a dead ship." The static cuts out abruptly, replaced by an ominous silence. Then, deep within the Meridian's darkened hull, running lights begin flickering to life in sequence.

"Well, gorram," you mutter, watching as the derelict transforms before your eyes. Weapon systems that should be offline start powering up, their targeting arrays sweeping toward Serenity. Zoe's hand moves to her sidearm. "Sir, I'm thinking whoever's over there wasn't exactly planning a welcome party."

Jayne chambers a round. "So much for the element of surprise." The Meridian's comm crackles to life with a cold, professional female voice: "Serenity, you are trespassing in a restricted military zone. Prepare to be boarded for inspection."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Try to bluff your way out, claiming you're just legitimate salvagers
B: Fire up Serenity's engines and attempt to escape immediately
C: Demand to know why an Alliance ship is posing as a derelict
D: Cut communications and prepare for a fight while looking for escape routes

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
"Alliance ships don't just play dead in the black," you say, your voice cutting through the tense silence on the bridge. "So why don't you explain why a perfectly functional military vessel is sitting here like bait in a trap?"

There's a long pause before the response crackles through. When it comes, the voice is different—colder, more clipped. "Captain Reynolds. Your reputation precedes you."

Zoe's hand moves instinctively to her weapon. "That ain't good," she mutters.

"The Meridian suffered a... containment breach," the voice continues, and you catch the slight hesitation. "We've been conducting salvage operations to secure sensitive materials before declaring the vessel a total loss. Your arrival has complicated matters."

Jayne snorts. "Containment breach, my ass. Alliance don't abandon ships worth this much coin unless something went real wrong."

Kaylee's fingers dance over her console, and her face goes pale. "Captain, I'm reading massive power signatures from their engines. This ship's been running hot this whole time—just dark. And those weapon locks... they've been tracking us since we dropped out of warp."

The pieces click together with uncomfortable clarity. This wasn't a salvage operation you stumbled into—it was a trap, and you just walked right into it.

A.text: Demand to know what kind of 'sensitive materials' require weapons lock on civilians
A.consequence: Pressing about sensitive materials triggers defensive response and increased threat level
A.narration_directive: The Alliance operative becomes evasive and hostile, revealing this involves classified human experimentation while escalating the standoff

B.text: Tell them you're leaving immediately and power up Serenity's engines
B.consequence: Attempting to leave triggers immediate weapons fire from the Meridian
B.narration_directive: The Alliance operative coldly informs you that leaving is not an option, and the Meridian's weapons power up with clear intent to fire

C.text: Ask why they knew your name and were expecting you specifically
C.consequence: Gains crucial information about how the Alliance tracked Serenity to this location
C.narration_directive: Your pointed question forces the operative to reveal more about their intelligence network and how they knew you'd take this job

D.text: Propose a deal—information about what really happened here in exchange for safe passage
D.consequence: Opens negotiations but reveals Serenity's crew knows more than the Alliance wants
D.narration_directive: The Alliance operative considers the proposal but becomes suspicious about what you might have already discovered aboard the Meridian

    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",

  },

    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option C.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Gains information about Alliance operations without escalating immediate threat"
Narration directive: "Your direct challenge catches the Alliance operative off-guard, revealing more about their mission than they intended to share."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
"Alliance ships don't just play dead in the black," you say, your voice cutting through the tense silence on the bridge. "So why don't you explain why a perfectly functional military vessel is sitting here like bait in a trap?"

There's a long pause before the response crackles through. When it comes, the voice is different—colder, more clipped. "Captain Reynolds. Your reputation precedes you."

Zoe's hand moves instinctively to her weapon. "That ain't good," she mutters.

"The Meridian suffered a... containment breach," the voice continues, and you catch the slight hesitation. "We've been conducting salvage operations to secure sensitive materials before declaring the vessel a total loss. Your arrival has complicated matters."

Jayne snorts. "Containment breach, my ass. Alliance don't abandon ships worth this much coin unless something went real wrong."

Kaylee's fingers dance over her console, and her face goes pale. "Captain, I'm reading massive power signatures from their engines. This ship's been running hot this whole time—just dark. And those weapon locks... they've been tracking us since we dropped out of warp."

The pieces click together with uncomfortable clarity. This wasn't a salvage operation you stumbled into—it was a trap, and you just walked right into it.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Demand to know what kind of 'sensitive materials' require weapons lock on civilians
B: Tell them you're leaving immediately and power up Serenity's engines
C: Ask why they knew your name and were expecting you specifically
D: Propose a deal—information about what really happened here in exchange for safe passage

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
The comm crackles with what might be Alliance-standard amusement. "Captain Reynolds, your reputation precedes you. Did you really think a salvage job this lucrative would just fall into your lap without strings attached?" The voice pauses, letting that sink in. "We've been tracking your financial difficulties for months. Your need for quick coin makes you... predictable."

Zoe's jaw tightens beside you, her hand resting near her sidearm. "Gorramn it, Mal," she mutters under her breath. "Should've known it was too good to be true."

Jayne shifts nervously, eyeing the weapons lock indicator. "So what, they just been waitin' for us to show up like some kinda welcome wagon?"

"More than that," the operative continues. "We seeded this job through three different intermediaries. Your fence on Persephone, the salvage broker on Santo, even that bartender you're so fond of on Beaumonde. Amazing how easy it is to guide someone when you know their weaknesses."

Kaylee's face goes pale. "They been watchin' us that long? That's... that's just creepy."

The weapon lock countdown shows fifty-eight seconds remaining, but something in the operative's tone suggests they're not quite ready to pull the trigger. They want something first.

A.text: Demand to know what they want from you and your crew
A.consequence: The operative reveals their true objective but becomes more aggressive, reducing negotiation time
A.narration_directive: The operative explains they want the research data and any witnesses eliminated, but grows impatient with Malcolm's directness

B.text: Try to buy time by asking about the research on the Meridian
B.consequence: Gains valuable intelligence about the Meridian's research while keeping the operative talking
B.narration_directive: The operative reveals disturbing details about the human experimentation aboard the Meridian, buying crucial time

C.text: Signal Wash to prepare evasive maneuvers while you keep them talking
C.consequence: Wash begins evasive preparations but the Alliance detects the activity, escalating the situation
C.narration_directive: Wash quietly begins prep work but the Meridian's sensors pick up engine fluctuations, making the operative suspicious

D.text: Bluff that you've already transmitted their location to Independent forces
D.consequence: The bluff backfires spectacularly as the Alliance calls it, accelerating their attack timeline
D.narration_directive: The operative laughs at the obvious bluff and cuts the weapon lock countdown in half, preparing to fire immediately

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Physical/mental states — injuries, poisons, buffs, curses, tasks in progress.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers, looming consequences, ticking time-bombs. Examples: pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming storms, spreading fires, political deadlines.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
The comm crackles with what might be Alliance-standard amusement. "Captain Reynolds, your reputation precedes you. Did you really think a salvage job this lucrative would just fall into your lap without strings attached?" The voice pauses, letting that sink in. "We've been tracking your financial difficulties for months. Your need for quick coin makes you... predictable."

Zoe's jaw tightens beside you, her hand resting near her sidearm. "Gorramn it, Mal," she mutters under her breath. "Should've known it was too good to be true."

Jayne shifts nervously, eyeing the weapons lock indicator. "So what, they just been waitin' for us to show up like some kinda welcome wagon?"

"More than that," the operative continues. "We seeded this job through three different intermediaries. Your fence on Persephone, the salvage broker on Santo, even that bartender you're so fond of on Beaumonde. Amazing how easy it is to guide someone when you know their weaknesses."

Kaylee's face goes pale. "They been watchin' us that long? That's... that's just creepy."

The weapon lock countdown shows fifty-eight seconds remaining, but something in the operative's tone suggests they're not quite ready to pull the trigger. They want something first.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Demand to know what they want from you and your crew
B: Try to buy time by asking about the research on the Meridian
C: Signal Wash to prepare evasive maneuvers while you keep them talking
D: Bluff that you've already transmitted their location to Independent forces

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Choice text: "Try to buy time by asking about the research on the Meridian"

OUTCOME CLASSIFICATION: ADVANCE_SAFE
Consequence: Gains valuable intelligence about the Meridian's research while keeping the operative talking
NARRATION DIRECTIVE: The operative reveals disturbing details about the human experimentation aboard the Meridian, buying crucial time
You MUST narrate this outcome exactly as classified. Do not soften, alter, or provide alternatives to the predetermined outcome.

data.passage: The Meridian's weapons charge with an ominous hum that vibrates through Serenity's hull, but you keep your voice steady. "Now hold on there. Before you go blowin' us to pieces, I'm curious about something. What exactly were you folk doing on that ship that's got the Alliance so worked up?" The comm crackles with static before Chen's voice returns, colder now but with a hint of pride. "Project Mindbridge. We were perfecting techniques for memory extraction and behavioral modification. The subjects were... volunteers from the outer colonies." Kaylee's face goes pale beside you. "Volunteers my gorram ass," Jayne mutters, but you wave him quiet. Chen continues, "The research was nearly complete when the facility suffered an... incident. We've been waiting for someone foolish enough to salvage the ship so we could test our field applications." Your blood runs cold as the implications sink in. "Field applications?" "On your crew, Captain Reynolds. Consider it a practical examination." The weapon lock indicator blinks faster now, but you've bought precious seconds and learned the horrifying truth. These aren't just Alliance spooks—they're the monsters who turned people into guinea pigs, and now they want to do the same to your family.

A.text: Warn the crew to prepare for combat while you try to break the weapon lock
A.consequence: Combat preparations begin but the weapon lock remains active, creating a dangerous standoff
A.narration_directive: The crew scrambles to battle stations while Malcolm works frantically to break the weapon lock, but Chen detects their preparations

B.text: Demand to know how many other crews they've used as test subjects
B.consequence: Gains more disturbing intelligence about the scope of Alliance experiments while maintaining the dialogue
B.narration_directive: Chen reveals the horrifying scale of their operations, providing crucial information about other victims while the weapon lock continues

C.text: Try to convince Chen that you can be more useful alive than dead
C.consequence: Chen becomes suspicious of Malcolm's motives and accelerates the weapon lock countdown
C.narration_directive: Chen sees through the manipulation attempt and decides to end the conversation, bringing the weapons to full charge

D.text: Cut communications and attempt an emergency burn away from the Meridian
D.consequence: Emergency burn begins but triggers immediate weapons fire from the Meridian
D.narration_directive: Serenity lurches into emergency acceleration as the Meridian opens fire, beginning a deadly chase sequence
